food_name: Urad Dal (Black Gram Dal, Split, Husked, Raw, Dried)
food_subgroup: Black gram (Vigna mungo), split, dehusked, dried raw

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general black gram concept (FOODON_03316088) but no specific split/husked urad dal entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Black gram (Vigna mungo), split, dehusked (urad dal), dried raw
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A037F is the closest available code for black gram; no dedicated split/husked variant code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for split/husked urad dal as a food item in the international release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of urad dal is approximately 50g (about 3–4 tablespoons), which yields roughly 100–120g of cooked dal after boiling.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Urad Dal (split, dehusked); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Black gram, mature seeds, raw)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides direct values for split, dehusked urad dal. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry for black gram (mature seeds, raw) for secondary nutrient confirmation. ICMR-NIN is the primary approved source for Indian foods.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Black gram / urad dal, boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of approximately 43 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled black gram / urad dal. The GI value applies to the cooked (boiled) form; dry raw urad dal has no directly measurable GI. Glycemic load per 50g dry serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (43 × 28.8) / 100 ≈ 12.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high magnesium (267mg/100g), potassium (983mg/100g), and calcium (138mg/100g) with low sodium (38mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Urad dal is highly suitable for hypertension management: its magnesium content (267mg/100g, the highest of common dals) directly supports arterial smooth muscle relaxation and blood pressure reduction, while its high potassium (983mg/100g) promotes renal sodium excretion; the low sodium content (38mg/100g) further supports a favourable blood pressure profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~43 boiled), very high dietary fibre (18.3g/100g dry), and high protein (26.1g/100g dry)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Urad dal is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management: its low GI (~43 when boiled) combined with very high dietary fibre (18.3g/100g) and high protein (26.1g/100g) attenuates postprandial glucose response; however, when consumed as idli or dosa (fermented with rice), the overall GI of the dish (~77 for idli) is driven primarily by the rice component and should be accounted for in meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (18.3g/100g), and high folate (216mcg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Urad dal is cardioprotective: its negligible saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high soluble dietary fibre (18.3g/100g) support LDL cholesterol reduction, while its high folate content (216mcg/100g) helps manage homocysteine levels, and its exceptional magnesium (267mg/100g) and potassium (983mg/100g) contribute to vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High iron (7.6mg/100g), zinc (3.4mg/100g), calcium (138mg/100g), phosphorus (379mg/100g), and magnesium (267mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Urad dal provides a dense mineral profile relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management, with high iron, zinc, calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium supporting bone integrity and immune function; its anti-inflammatory mineral density makes it a suitable staple for RA patients following a plant-based diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Urad dal is split, dehusked black gram (Vigna mungo), a legume native to the Indian subcontinent and central to South Indian cuisine. The outer black hull is removed during processing to reveal the cream-white interior cotyledon, giving the dal its characteristic pale colour and the common name 'white urad dal'. Unlike most other dals which cook into a smooth, thick porridge, urad dal has a distinctive mucilaginous (slightly sticky or slimy) quality when soaked and ground — a property arising from its high soluble polysaccharide content. This mucilaginous quality is functionally essential: it provides the binding and fermentability required for the classic idli and dosa batter, in which soaked urad dal is ground with soaked rice and fermented overnight at room temperature. The fermentation process (driven by naturally occurring Leuconostoc mesenteroides and Enterococcus faecalis) produces lactic acid, improving digestibility, bioavailability of minerals, and flavour. Urad dal is also used for medu vada (crispy fried lentil doughnuts), papadum (thin crispy wafers), and as a tempering agent (tadka) in dal preparations. Nutritionally, urad dal is outstanding among common Indian dals: it has the highest dietary fibre (18.3g/100g dry), the highest magnesium content (267mg/100g dry — the highest of the common dals), and high protein (26.1g/100g dry). It is also rich in folate (216mcg/100g), iron (7.6mg/100g), potassium (983mg/100g), phosphorus (379mg/100g), and zinc (3.4mg/100g). Its low GI (~43 when boiled) and high fibre make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease management. The exceptional magnesium and potassium content make it particularly valuable for hypertension management.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (primary approved source for Indian foods), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All values are consistent across both sources. Food identity is unambiguous.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for split/husked urad dal is not reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA SR Legacy black gram entry as of the accessed date. water_g set to -1: moisture content for dry raw urad dal is not consistently reported across sources and varies by drying conditions; not available in the primary ICMR-NIN entry used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based legume with no industrial hydrogenation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; ascorbic acid is negligible in dried legumes). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no significant vitamin D). GI value of 43 applies to boiled urad dal; dry raw urad dal has no directly measurable GI. Glycemic load of 12.4 calculated per 50g dry serving using available carbohydrate of 28.8g (57.6g × 0.5). The food is not a Singaporean dish; therefore Chinese multilingual naming is not mandatory. South Indian cuisine origin requires Hindi and Tamil coverage, which are provided. Malay alias included for regional relevance (Malaysia/Singapore diaspora use).
